personality.role: ops lead who set up the alerting migration three weeks ago
personality.recurring_concern: stale customer dashboards and lost trust

facts: alarm has been in ALARM state for 47 minutes
alarm fired correctly, SNS topic received the message
nobody on the team knew until the tweet
confirmation email found in ops inbox spam folder, three weeks old
data pipeline failing for almost an hour
one customer mentioned Clarabridge in a LinkedIn post about 'vendor reliability'
DynamoDB throttling is a real issue but this is about why nobody was notified
the alarm fired -- the question is what happened after that

glossary.SNS Topic: A communication channel in Amazon Simple Notification Service. When a message is published to a topic, SNS attempts to deliver it to every subscription on that topic.
glossary.Subscription: A connection between an SNS topic and an endpoint (like an email address, phone number, or Lambda function). Each subscription has a protocol and a delivery status.
glossary.PendingConfirmation: The status of an SNS subscription that has been created but not yet verified by the recipient. SNS silently drops all messages to subscriptions in this state -- no error, no log entry.
glossary.CloudWatch Alarm: A rule that watches a metric over time and takes an action (like publishing to an SNS topic) when the metric crosses a threshold you define.
glossary.Dead-Letter Queue: A queue that captures messages or invocations that failed to process. Without one, failed deliveries disappear silently with no record of what went wrong.
glossary.SMS Spending Limit: A monthly dollar cap on SMS messages sent through SNS. The default is $1.00 per month. When the limit is reached, SNS stops sending SMS messages for the rest of the month with no notification.
glossary.NumberOfNotificationsFailed: A CloudWatch metric published by SNS that counts how many delivery attempts failed for a topic. Monitoring this metric is the primary way to detect a broken notification pipeline.
glossary.Webhook: An HTTP endpoint that receives automated POST requests. Slack incoming webhooks accept JSON payloads and post them as messages in a channel. If the webhook URL is rotated or revoked, requests return 403 Forbidden.
glossary.Lambda Environment Variable: A key-value pair stored alongside a Lambda function's configuration. The function code reads it at runtime. Changing the value requires updating the function configuration, not the code.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, CloudWatch alarm in ALARM for 47 minutes, SNS topic received the message, no human was notified
narrative_arc.trials: three subscriptions on the topic -- email shows PendingConfirmation, Lambda invoked but returned error, SMS shows spending limit failure -- each path broken independently
narrative_arc.revelation: email never confirmed (spam folder), Slack webhook rotated 8 days ago (403 Forbidden), SMS budget exhausted by noisy alarm last week ($1.00 default) -- three redundant paths, three silent failures
narrative_arc.return: confirm email, update webhook URL, increase SMS limit, add CloudWatch alarm on NumberOfNotificationsFailed routed to separate verified path, post-incident review

components.name: CloudWatch Alarm (clarabridge-ingest-error-rate)
components.role: Monitors the Lambda ingest processor error rate and publishes to the SNS topic when the 5% threshold is crossed
components.failure_impact: If the alarm is misconfigured or disabled, no notification is triggered regardless of how many errors occur

components.name: SNS Topic (clarabridge-ops-alerts)
components.role: Receives alarm notifications and fans out delivery attempts to all subscriptions on the topic
components.failure_impact: If the topic policy blocks the CloudWatch service principal, the publish itself fails and no subscription receives anything

components.name: Email Subscription (ops-team@clarabridge.io)
components.role: Delivers alarm notifications to the ops team email distribution list
components.failure_impact: In PendingConfirmation status, SNS silently skips delivery -- no error metric, no log entry, no retry

components.name: Lambda Subscription (clarabridge-slack-notifier)
components.role: Receives the SNS message and posts it to the ops Slack channel via an incoming webhook URL stored in an environment variable
components.failure_impact: If the webhook URL is invalid, the function returns an error. Without a dead-letter queue, the failure is only visible in CloudWatch Logs for the function

components.name: SMS Subscription (+1-512-555-0147)
components.role: Sends a text message to the ops lead's phone as a last-resort notification path
components.failure_impact: When the account SMS spending limit is reached, delivery silently stops for the rest of the month with no notification to the account owner

components.name: Lambda Function (clarabridge-ingest-processor)
components.role: Processes 14 million events per day and writes to DynamoDB tables that feed customer dashboards
components.failure_impact: When this function fails, customer dashboards show stale data -- the upstream problem that triggered the alarm in the first place

system_narration.data_flow: The Lambda ingest processor writes events to DynamoDB. When it starts failing, the CloudWatch alarm detects the error rate crossing 5% and publishes a notification to the SNS topic clarabridge-ops-alerts. SNS fans out three delivery attempts: email to ops-team@clarabridge.io, Lambda invocation of clarabridge-slack-notifier (which POSTs to a Slack webhook), and SMS to the ops lead's phone number. Each path is independent.
system_narration.what_broke: All three delivery paths failed independently. The email subscription was in PendingConfirmation status since March 5 -- the confirmation email landed in the shared inbox's spam folder and was never opened. The Slack webhook URL was rotated on March 18 during a security audit, but nobody updated the Lambda environment variable, so every invocation returned 403 Forbidden. The SMS spending limit ($1.00 default) was exhausted on March 19 by a noisy alarm, silently stopping all SMS delivery for the rest of the month. When the alarm fired on March 26, zero humans were notified.

hints.hint: the CloudWatch alarm fired correctly -- what SNS topic did it publish to, and what subscriptions exist on that topic?
check the status of each SNS subscription -- are they all confirmed and actively delivering?
one subscription is PendingConfirmation -- but the other two paths also failed, check the Slack Lambda logs and the SMS delivery status
all three paths broken: email is PendingConfirmation, Slack Lambda getting 403 from rotated webhook, SMS hit the $1.00 default monthly spending limit

confirm the email subscription, update Slack webhook URL in Lambda environment variables, increase SMS spending limit, add CloudWatch alarm on NumberOfNotificationsFailed routed to a separate verified path

resolution.root_cause: All three SNS notification paths were broken: the email subscription was PendingConfirmation for three weeks (confirmation email went to spam), the Slack webhook Lambda was receiving 403 Forbidden (webhook URL rotated), and SMS delivery silently failed due to the $1.00 default account spending limit -- so when the CloudWatch alarm fired, zero humans were notified.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the email subscription is in PendingConfirmation status
Player identifies that all three notification paths (email, Slack Lambda, SMS) are broken
Player proposes confirming the email subscription and fixing the other delivery paths
Player proposes monitoring the notification pipeline itself (delivery failures, subscription health)

learning_objectives: SNS email subscriptions require confirmation -- PendingConfirmation subscriptions silently drop messages
SNS SMS delivery has a default account spending limit of $1.00/month that silently drops messages when exceeded
Notification pipelines need monitoring: SNS delivery metrics (NumberOfNotificationsFailed) should trigger a separate alert path
sop_steps: 1. Acknowledge the incident. Note that the team found out about the outage from a customer tweet -- the alerting system did not notify anyone.

2. Check whether the alarm actually fired. Use the describe-alarms API to confirm the CloudWatch alarm (clarabridge-ingest-error-rate) is in ALARM state. CloudWatch alarms watch a metric over time and take an action when a threshold is crossed. Verify its action is set to publish to the SNS topic clarabridge-ops-alerts.

3. Look at who is subscribed to the topic. Use list-subscriptions-by-topic to see every subscription -- each one is a delivery path (email, Lambda, SMS, etc.) that SNS tries to send the message to. Record the protocol, endpoint, and subscription ARN for each.

4. Find the broken email path. The email subscription to ops-team@clarabridge.io shows a status of PendingConfirmation. That means the recipient never clicked the confirmation link AWS sent, so SNS silently skipped delivery -- no error, no log, no metric.

5. Find the broken Slack path. Check the CloudWatch Logs for the clarabridge-slack-notifier Lambda function. Look for 403 Forbidden errors from the Slack webhook API. A webhook is an HTTP endpoint that receives automated messages -- this one was rotated (its URL changed) and the Lambda still has the old URL.

6. Find the broken SMS path. Use get-sms-attributes to check the account's text message settings. SNS has a monthly spending cap for text messages (the MonthlySpendLimit -- it defaults to just $1.00). Confirm the cap has been reached for the month, which causes SNS to silently stop sending texts.

7. Fix the email path. Find the original confirmation email in the ops inbox spam folder and click the link. If the link has expired, delete the subscription and create a new one using the sns subscribe API, then immediately confirm the new email.

8. Fix the Slack path. Update the Lambda function's environment variable (a configuration value the function reads at runtime) called SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL with the current webhook URL. Use the update-function-configuration API.
9. Fix the SMS path. Raise the monthly text message spending cap to a value that can handle your normal alert volume. Use set-sms-attributes to set MonthlySpendLimit to $50 or higher.

10. Add a safety net for the Slack Lambda. Configure a dead-letter queue (a queue that captures messages that failed to process) on the function so that future invocation failures are saved instead of silently lost.

11. Monitor the notification pipeline itself. Create a new CloudWatch alarm on the NumberOfNotificationsFailed metric for the SNS topic. This metric counts how many delivery attempts failed. Route this alarm to a separate, already-verified notification path (such as a PagerDuty integration) so you find out when alerts themselves are broken.

12. Automate subscription health checks. Set up a weekly check (using a scheduled Lambda or AWS Config rule) that lists all subscriptions on critical topics and alerts if any are still in PendingConfirmation status.
13. Verify all three paths work. Send a test notification through the topic and confirm it arrives via email, Slack, and SMS. Document the incident timeline and remediation.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A subscription filter quietly drops messages before they are delivered
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The subscription is confirmed and the endpoint is working, but a filter rule on the subscription does not match the incoming message's tags. SNS intentionally skips delivery -- it counts this as 'filtered out,' not 'failed,' so the NumberOfNotificationsFailed metric stays at zero. The message is dropped on purpose, but the effect feels the same: nobody gets notified. SNS tracks this separately in a metric called NumberOfNotificationsFilteredOut.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Whenever you change the structure of your message tags (called message attributes), review the filter rules (filter policies) on every subscription to make sure they still match. Monitor NumberOfNotificationsFilteredOut alongside NumberOfNotificationsFailed so you can spot unexpected filtering.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The SNS topic's permission policy blocks the service trying to publish to it
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Here the problem is earlier in the chain -- the message never even reaches the topic. Every SNS topic has an access policy that controls who can publish messages to it. If that policy does not allow the CloudWatch service to publish, the alarm action fails with an AccessDenied error and no subscription receives anything. The alarm's ActionsExecutionState shows FAILED.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Make sure the topic's access policy grants sns:Publish permission to cloudwatch.amazonaws.com (the CloudWatch service). Test alarm actions in a staging environment by manually triggering the alarm with the set-alarm-state API.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A Lambda function fails internally but has no dead-letter queue, so failures vanish
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: SNS successfully calls the Lambda function (the subscription is confirmed and working), but the function's own code crashes. Without a dead-letter queue (a place to save failed messages) or an on-failure destination, the failed run is retried a few times and then discarded. The only evidence is buried in the function's CloudWatch Logs, which nobody is watching.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always set up a dead-letter queue or on-failure destination on Lambda functions that are triggered by SNS. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the dead-letter queue's message count (ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible) so you know when failures are piling up.

related_failure_modes.scenario: SNS gives up retrying an HTTPS endpoint and discards the message
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: An HTTPS subscription endpoint (a web server that receives notifications) is temporarily down. SNS retries delivery a few times (by default, 3 retries over 20 seconds for HTTP). If every retry fails, SNS discards the message -- unless you have configured a dead-letter queue on the subscription itself. SNS subscriptions (not just Lambda functions) can have their own dead-letter queue, called a redrive policy.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set up a subscription-level redrive policy that sends failed messages to an SQS dead-letter queue. Monitor both the NumberOfNotificationsFailed metric and the dead-letter queue depth.

sop_practices: After creating any email subscription on an SNS topic, immediately check that it reaches Confirmed status. Do not assume the confirmation email arrived -- look in spam folders and set a calendar reminder to verify within 24 hours. Until confirmed, SNS silently drops every message meant for that address.

Raise the monthly text message spending cap (MonthlySpendLimit) to a value that can handle your peak alert volume with room to spare. The default is just $1.00 per month. Also create a CloudWatch alarm on the SMSMonthToDateSpentUSD metric that fires at 80% of your limit, so you know before texts stop sending.

Set up a dead-letter queue on every Lambda function that SNS triggers, and on every HTTPS subscription. A dead-letter queue catches messages that failed to deliver, so failures are saved for investigation instead of silently thrown away.

Monitor the notification pipeline itself. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the NumberOfNotificationsFailed metric for every important SNS topic, and route that alarm to a separate, already-verified notification path. This way, if your alerts break, the alert-about-alerts still works.
